answer:
classic david v goliath — stop playing checklist game, u can't win it & don't need to

judo moves:

1. **reframe the buying criteria.** checklists r the incumbent's home turf. change the question from "who has most features" to "who solves ur specific job fastest." pick 1-2 dimensions u actually win (speed to 1st value, DX, price/seat, single use case done 10x better) & hammer only those. if u let the eval rubric stand, u already lost b4 demo day

2. **weaponize their breadth.** every feature they have = complexity, config, admin overhead, training, bloat. positioning: "they're built for procurement, we're built for the ppl who actually use it daily." feature count becomes THEIR liability

3. **niche down publicly.** "best X for [segment/stack/workflow]" beats "alternative to BigCo." incumbents can't chase every niche w/o diluting. u want buyers to feel checklist comparison is category error — like comparing scalpel to swiss army knife

4. **change who ur selling to.** bake-offs w/ checklists = top-down procurement, incumbent's game. go bottoms-up: free tier / PLG so devs already love u b4 eval starts. best judo = never enter the bake-off, arrive as the tool the team already uses

5. **rig future bake-offs.** publish ur own eval guide / comparison page w/ criteria u win (time-to-hello-world, p95 latency, support response time, TCO). champions will crib from it. give ur internal champ ammo: 1-pager on why checklist ≠ outcomes

6. **proof > claims.** head-to-head timed demo ("set up both, we'll be live in 15 min, they won't"), migration case studies w/ hard #s (cut build time 40%, dropped 3 tools)

tactical: next bake-off, ask buyer "which of these features did ur team use last month in current tool?" — usually <20%. shifts convo from coverage to usage instantly

biggest mistake to avoid: roadmap catch-up promises. validates their frame & u'll always be behind. own the gap: "we don't do X on purpose, here's why that helps u"
